coredata.dc:title: Dual-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection of cholesterol using a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alsharif, Shaker T.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: This work introduces a novel approach to cholesterol detection using a fluorometric method that incorporates a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system. The method employs a ratiometric detection strategy that leverages the enzymatic reaction between cholesterol and cholesterol oxidase, resulting in the production of H2O2. This H2O2 subsequently oxidizes cysteine, leading to the release of Zn2+ ion from their cysteine (CYS) complexes. The liberated Zn2+ ion interact differentially with two types of CDs, simultaneously decreasing the emission intensity of blue CDs (BCDs) at 390 nm while increasing the emission intensity of red CDs (RCDs) at 600 nm. This dual-signal change enables ratiometric detection, significantly enhancing the method’s sensitivity and reliability. In-depth characterization of the CDs and study of their interaction mechanisms were achieved through the application of various spectroscopic and morphological methods. The proposed method demonstrated high sensitivity (limit of detection = 0.014 μM) and excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9952) over a low linearity range (0.05–1.0 μM) of cholesterol concentrations. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied to human serum samples, yielding excellent recovery values (97.00 % to 98.67 %), thus validating its potential for practical clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cholesterol
Ratiometric sensing
Zinc-cysteine complex
Cholesterol oxidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The calculation of quantum yields of CDs
Sensing of cholesterol in serum samples
Detection of cholesterol in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and size distribution histogram of (A) BCDs and (B) RCDs, FTIR spectrum of (C) BCDs and (D) RCDs, XPS spectrum of (E) BCDs and (F) RCDs.

(A) UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra of BCDs, fluorescent emission spectra of BCDs with the characteristic peak at 600 nm (blue line) after excitation at 287 nm (red line). UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra of BCDs, fluorescent emission spectra of BCDs with the characteristic peak at 390 nm (blue line) after excitation at 290 nm (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis spectra overlay of BCDs and (BCDs + Zn2+), (B) Three Stern-Volmer curves at different temperatures (333 K, 313 K, and 298 K), (C) UV–Vis spectra overlay of RCDs and (RCDs + Zn2+), and (D) Zeta potential of RCDs and (RCDs + Zn2+).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system as a function of concentration of cholesterol. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of cholesterol in the range of 0–1.0 µM.
Comparison of fluorescence responses of CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase sensor towards cholesterol and other interferents.
A comparison between this study and other established fluorescent probes for cholesterol detection.
Determination of cholesterol in human serum samples via the proposed and reported methods (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The tabulated values of t-test at p = 0.05.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The tabulated values of F test at p = 0.05.

head.ce:title: Dual-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection of cholesterol using a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Science Department, College of Pharmacy, Umm AlQura University, Makkah 21955, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Science Department, College of Pharmacy, Umm AlQura University, Makkah 21955, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work introduces a novel approach to cholesterol detection using a fluorometric method that incorporates a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system. The method employs a ratiometric detection strategy that leverages the enzymatic reaction between cholesterol and cholesterol oxidase, resulting in the production of H2O2. This H2O2 subsequently oxidizes cysteine, leading to the release of Zn2+ ion from their cysteine (CYS) complexes. The liberated Zn2+ ion interact differentially with two types of CDs, simultaneously decreasing the emission intensity of blue CDs (BCDs) at 390 nm while increasing the emission intensity of red CDs (RCDs) at 600 nm. This dual-signal change enables ratiometric detection, significantly enhancing the method’s sensitivity and reliability. In-depth characterization of the CDs and study of their interaction mechanisms were achieved through the application of various spectroscopic and morphological methods. The proposed method demonstrated high sensitivity (limit of detection = 0.014 μM) and excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9952) over a low linearity range (0.05–1.0 μM) of cholesterol concentrations. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied to human serum samples, yielding excellent recovery values (97.00 % to 98.67 %), thus validating its potential for practical clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol, a crucial lipid molecule, plays a vital role in maintaining cellular structure, producing hormones, and synthesizing bile acids for digestion. However, elevated cholesterol levels, especially low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, can result in significant health problems, including atherosclerosis, stroke, and heart disease [1]. In normal conditions, the cholesterol concentration in human serum should be below 200 mg dL−1, with levels exceeding 240 mg dL−1 indicating high blood cholesterol [2]. Levels above this range significantly increase cardiovascular risk. Conversely, hypocholesterolemia, characterized by a low cholesterol concentration (<167 mg dL−1), can lead to hemorrhagic stroke [3]. The accurate determination of cholesterol levels is of paramount importance in clinical settings, as it allows for early detection of hypercholesterolemia, assessment of cardiovascular risk, monitoring of treatment efficacy, and implementation of preventive measures. Regular cholesterol testing enables healthcare providers to tailor interventions, including lifestyle modifications and pharmacological treatments, to efficiently regulate cholesterol levels and lower the chances of associated health complications.

Various analytical methods have been employed for the determination of cholesterol, each with its own strengths and limitations. Thin layer chromatography (TLC) offers simplicity but lacks precision for quantitative analysis [4]. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) provides excellent separation and sensitivity but requires expensive equipment and expertise [5]. Electrochemical methods offer rapid analysis but can suffer from interference from other electroactive species in biological samples [6]. Colorimetric methods are widely used due to their simplicity and low cost, but they may lack specificity and can be affected by interfering substances [7]. Fluorometric techniques offer high sensitivity but may require sample derivatization and can be influenced by matrix effects [8]. These traditional methods, while useful, often face challenges such as time-consuming sample preparation, limited selectivity, potential for interference from other biomolecules, and in some cases, the need for large sample volumes or expensive instrumentation. Additionally, many of these techniques struggle with direct analysis in complex biological matrices, necessitating extensive sample clean-up procedures. The limitations of these methods have driven the search for more robust, sensitive, and selective approaches for cholesterol determination, particularly for applications in clinical diagnostics and biomedical research.

Fluorescence-based methods have emerged as powerful tools for the detection and quantification of biological molecules, offering high sensitivity, selectivity, and real-time monitoring capabilities [9–11]. Carbon dots (CDs) stand out among the various fluorescent probes that have gained notable attention in recent years because of their remarkable properties and multifunctional roles in biosensing [12,13]. These nanoscale carbon-based materials exhibit excellent photoluminescence, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization, making them ideal candidates for biological detection [14,15]. CDs can be synthesized from a wide range of precursors using simple and cost-effective methods, resulting in tunable optical properties that can be tailored for specific target molecules [16]. In biological sensing applications, carbon dots have been successfully employed for the detection of various molecules including proteins, nucleic acids, small metabolites, and even whole cells or microorganisms [17].

The proposed fluorometric method presents a novel and innovative approach for cholesterol detection using a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system, offering several unique advantages. This ratiometric detection strategy ingeniously exploits the enzymatic reaction of cholesterol oxidase with cholesterol, which liberates H2O2. The subsequent oxidation of cysteine by H2O2 triggers the release of Zn2+ ion from their complex with cysteine. The liberated Zn2+ ion then interact differentially with two types of CDs, causing a simultaneous increase in the fluorescence intensity of BCDs at 390 nm and a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of RCDs at 600 nm. This dual-signal change allows for ratiometric detection, enhancing the method's sensitivity and reliability. The use of CDs as fluorescent probes offers benefits such as low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties. The ratiometric nature of the detection minimizes the influence of environmental factors and improves the accuracy of measurements. Furthermore, the successful application of this method to human serum samples demonstrates its practical utility in clinical settings, potentially offering a more sensitive, specific, and robust alternative to traditional cholesterol detection methods. This innovative approach combines the specificity of enzymatic reactions with the sensitivity of fluorescence spectroscopy, presenting a promising tool for precise cholesterol quantification in complex biological matrices.

Cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase (ChOx, 100 U/mL from Streptomyces sp.), methyl resorcinol, ethylenediamine, p-aminobenzoic acid, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were supplied by Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. Ascorbic acid, galactose, glucose, L-valine, boric acid, L-tryptophan, glutathione, L-methionine, phosphoric acid, L-arginine, L-cysteine, L-histidine, L-lysine, L-threonine, acetic acid, urea, uric acid, KCl, NaCl, NH4Cl, ZnCl2, and CaCl2 were all purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. All reagents used were sourced commercially and of analytical grade, applied directly without any need for additional purification.
Refer to Supplementary data file.

The synthesis of RCDs and BCDs was carried out using distinct precursor combinations and a hydrothermal method. For RCDs, 0.11 g of 3-methylresorcinol and 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine were used, while BCDs were prepared from 0.012 g of p-aminobenzoic acid and 0.02 g of o-phenylenediamine (OPD). The respective precursor mixtures were initially stirred at room temperature for 5 min to ensure thorough mixing. Subsequently, each reaction mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and subjected to heating at 180 °C for 6 h in a hot air oven. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the synthesized solutions underwent centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove any solid precipitates. The supernatants were purified by dialysis over a 48-hour period using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da to eliminate unreacted precursors and small molecular byproducts. Finally, the purified carbon dot solutions were freeze-dried to obtain the respective red and blue CDs in powder form, ready for further characterization and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation of quantum yields of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Details on the procedures and calculations for assessing the quantum yield are provided in the Supplementary data file.

The following method for synthesizing a zinc-cysteine complex is based on established techniques reported in prior studies [18]. Dissolve zinc chloride (ZnCl2) in deionized water to create a 50 mM solution. In a separate container, prepare a 100 mM cysteine solution by dissolving L-cysteine in deionized water. Combine these solutions in 1:2 ratio of Zn2+ ion to cysteine and mix thoroughly. Following established protocols, adjust the pH of the resulting solution to 7.0 by carefully adding 2 M NaOH dropwise.

Initially, 100 μL of cholesterol solution (at different concentrations) was mixed with 50 μL of cholesterol oxidase (20 U/mL) in 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). This mixture was incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 25 min. Subsequently, 100 μL each of (red CDs, 5.0 mg/mL), (blue CDs, 3.0 mg/mL), and 1.0 mL of CYS@Zn complex were added to the reaction mixture. The solution was then diluted to a final volume of 10.0 mL using 10.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) and incubated for an additional 5 min. The excitation wavelength of 290 nm was employed to record the fluorescence spectra. The ratiometric fluorescence signal, calculated as the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 600 nm and 390 nm (F600/F390), was used for the quantitative determination of cholesterol, providing a sensitive and reliable measure of cholesterol concentration in the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of cholesterol in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the detection method's applicability to serum samples, blood was collected from three individuals (10 mL each) and processed to obtain serum. The blood samples were left at room temperature for 1 h, after which the uppermost layer was carefully transferred to clean test tubes and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min. For fluorescence detection of cholesterol, the resulting serum was diluted with normal saline at a ratio of 1:1000. To accurately quantify cholesterol levels in these serum samples, a standard addition method was employed. This involved adding known concentrations of cholesterol (0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 μM) to aliquots of the diluted serum and repeating the detection procedure for each spiked sample. This comprehensive approach allowed for the assessment of the method's performance in complex biological matrices and provided a reliable means to quantify cholesterol levels in human serum samples, demonstrating the practical utility of the developed fluorometric method.

TEM analysis provided insights into the morphology and size distribution of BCDs and RCDs, as presented in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1B. The images demonstrate that both types of CDs exhibit a spherical morphology. According to size measurements, the average diameter of BCDs is 4.4 nm, while RCDs have a larger average diameter of 5.8 nm (shown in the insets of Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B). The XRD patterns of the synthesized CDs provided further structural insights. As presented in Fig. S1A, the XRD pattern of the BCDs exhibits an intense diffraction peak at a 2θ angle of 21.4°, which is typically associated with the (002) plane. Similarly, the XRD pattern of the RCDs, shown in Fig. S1B, demonstrates a diffraction peak at a 2θ angle of 20.5°, which can be assigned to the same (002) plane. These XRD results confirm the graphitic-like crystalline structure of both types of CDs, indicating the successful synthesis of these carbon-based nanomaterials [19]. The FTIR spectra of both BCDs and RCDs reveal their surface functional groups and provide insights into their structure. For BCDs (Fig. 1C), prepared from p-aminobenzoic acid and o-phenylenediamine, the spectrum shows a broad peak at 3391 cm−1 (N–H and O–H stretching), small peaks at 2821 cm−1 and 2060 cm−1 (C–H stretching), a strong peak at 1588 cm−1 (C=C aromatic stretching or N–H bending), peaks at 1361 cm−1 (C–N stretching) and 1091 cm−1 (C–O stretching), and a peak at 762 cm−1 (aromatic C–H bending) [20]. For RCDs (Fig. 1D), synthesized from 3-methylresorcinol and ethylenediamine, the spectrum exhibits a broad peak at 3128 cm−1 (N–H and O–H stretching), peaks at 1660 cm−1 and 1609 cm−1 (C=C aromatic stretching or N–H bending), a peak at 1397 cm−1 (C–N stretching), and small peaks at 1120 cm−1 (C–O stretching), 733 cm−1, and 631 cm−1 (aromatic C–H bending) [21]. These spectral features confirm the presence of various functional groups, including amino, hydroxyl, and aromatic moieties, on the surface of both types of carbon dots, which contribute to their unique properties. XPS analysis of both BCDs and RCDs provided valuable information about their elemental composition and chemical states. The BCDs exhibited three distinct peaks (Fig. 1E): one at 283.65 eV, corresponding to the C 1s orbital; another at 399.84 eV, attributed to the N 1s orbital; and a third at 529.01 eV, associated with the O 1s orbital. Similarly, the RCDs showed three peaks at slightly different binding energies (Fig. 1F): 282.44 eV (C 1s), 394.25 eV (N 1s), and 528.05 eV (O 1s) [22]. These results confirm the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in both types of CDs, consistent with their synthesis from nitrogen-containing precursors.

The optical properties of both BCDs and RCDs were thoroughly investigated using UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, BCDs exhibited a distinct UV–vis absorption peak at 223 nm, attributed to the π → π* electronic excitations of sp2 carbon in aromatic structures, and another peak at 283 nm, assigned to the n → π* electronic excitations of C═O and C═N groups [23]. BCDs showed a dependence of emission on excitation wavelength, with the emission wavelength red shifting as the excitation wavelength ranged from 240 to 320 nm (Fig. S3A). The highest emission from BCDs occurred at 390 nm under 290 nm excitation, resulting in a fluorescence quantum yield of 26.14 %. In contrast, RCDs (Fig. 2B) showed a maximum absorption peak at 289 nm, attributed to π → π* electronic transitions, with no visible light region absorption, possibly due to quantum size effects [24,25]. RCDs displayed excitation-independent fluorescence behavior, maintaining a constant emission wavelength at 600 nm across various excitation wavelengths (250–330 nm), as demonstrated in Fig. S2B. The fluorescence excitation peak for RCDs was at 290 nm, and they exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield of 25.3 %.

The stability of both BCDs and RCDs was extensively evaluated under various environmental conditions. As shown in Fig. S2A, BCDs maintained stable fluorescence intensity across a wide pH range (5–11), with a decrease observed only under highly acidic conditions (pH 2–4). RCDs exhibited a narrower stability range (pH 5–9), with fluorescence intensity diminishing in both highly acidic (pH 2–4) and basic (pH 10–11) environments, likely ascribed to protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups. Both types of carbon dots demonstrated remarkable salt tolerance, maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity even at NaCl concentrations up to 3.0 M (Fig. S2B). Photobleaching resistance was evident as the photoluminescence of both CDs remained unchanged under UV irradiation (365 nm) for extended periods up to 700 min (Fig. S2C). Temperature stability tests revealed that fluorescence intensity remained constant for both CDs up to 60 °C, beyond which a gradual decrease was observed (Fig. S2D). The decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs above 60 °C can be attributed to several factors. Elevated temperatures lead to thermal quenching, where increased molecular vibrations promote non-radiative relaxation pathways, reducing fluorescence emission. Heat can also affect the structural integrity of the CDs, causing alterations in the carbon core and surface passivation layers that disrupt electronic structures responsible for fluorescence [26]. Additionally, changes in surface states and functional groups occur at higher temperatures, affecting the fluorescence efficiency due to the alteration of surface chemistry. Heat-induced electron transfer processes become more prevalent, with excited electrons transferring to surrounding molecules like oxygen, further diminishing fluorescence. Elevated temperatures may also promote the aggregation of carbon dots, leading to fluorescence quenching through mechanisms such as energy transfer between closely packed dots [27]. These comprehensive stability studies highlight the excellent pH stability, salt tolerance, photobleaching resistance, and thermal stability of the prepared CDs, underscoring their reliability and durability for various applications, including sensing and bioimaging.

The optimization of reaction conditions plays a great role in enhancing the sensitivity of the proposed cholesterol sensor. While previous studies have established optimal conditions for cholesterol oxidase activity (pH 7.4, 37 °C, 25-minute incubation) [28], our focus was on fine-tuning three critical parameters for maximum efficiency in biological sample analysis: pH, incubation time, and CYS@Zn volume. We utilized the fluorescence intensity ratio F600/F390 (emission at 600 nm/emission at 390 nm) as our performance metric. Our pH optimization study, conducted over a range of 5.0–10.0 (Fig. S4A). The F600/F390 ratio peaked at pH 7.0, which was subsequently chosen for further experiments. The observed decline in fluorescence ratio at higher pH levels may be attributed to Zn2+ ion precipitation in alkaline environments. Incubation time optimization (Fig. S4B) showed a gradual increase in the F600/F390 ratio, reaching a plateau after approximately 5.0 min. To ensure signal stability and maximize the fluorescence ratio, we adopted this 5.0-minute incubation period for subsequent analyses. Lastly, we examined the effect of CYS@Zn volume on sensor response (Fig. S4C). The F600/F390 ratio exhibited a steady increase with increasing CYS@Zn volume, stabilizing at 1.0 mL. The stabilization observed at 1.0 mL indicates that this volume represents the point where we achieve optimal stoichiometric balance between the CYS@Zn complex and the CDs. At this point, we have sufficient Zn2+ ions to saturate all available binding sites on both BCDs and RCDs. Any additional CYS@Zn beyond 1.0 mL does not contribute to further changes in the fluorescence ratio because all possible interaction sites are already occupied, creating a plateau effect in our measurements. Based on these findings, we established the optimal conditions for our cholesterol detection method in biological samples: pH 7.0, 5.0-minute incubation time, and 1.0 mL CYS@Zn volume. These parameters were employed in all subsequent experiments to ensure the most sensitive and reliable cholesterol quantification.

The method utilizes a system involving cholesterol oxidase, cysteine, Zn2+, and two types of CDs (BCDs and RCDs). The process begins with cholesterol oxidase catalyzing the conversion of cholesterol, which produces H2O2 as a by-product. This H2O2 then oxidizes cysteine, converting it to its disulfide form. As a result, Zn2+ ions are released from their complexes with cysteine. The liberated Zn2+ ions then interact with the BCDs and RCDs present in the system. This interaction with the Zn2+ ions triggers a dual fluorescence response. On one hand, it leads to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm, which is associated with the BCDs. On the other hand, it causes an increase in the fluorescence at 600 nm, which is linked to the RCDs. By monitoring these contrasting fluorescence changes, the method can enable sensitive and specific detection of the target analyte, which in this case is cholesterol. The underlying mechanisms responsible for these observed effects will be further elaborated in the following sections.

The BCDs were not influenced by the presence of H2O2, cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase, or cysteine, as shown in Fig. S5A. This ensured that any observed effects could be attributed to the interaction between the BCDs and the Zn2+ ions. Utilizing UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, the researchers noted a broadening and reduction in the intensity of the BCDs' absorption peak at 283 nm upon the addition of Zn2+ (Fig. 3 A). This observation suggests the formation of a new complex, which could be attributed to the chelation between the functional groups of the BCDs and the Zn2+ ions. Specifically, the oxygen atoms in the –COOH groups and the nitrogen atom in the –NH2 groups of the BCDs are believed to coordinate with the Zn2+ ions by donating their lone pair electrons, leading to the observed fluorescence quenching. To further investigate the detection mechanism, zeta potential measurements were conducted. The initial large negative potential of the BCDs (−13.5 mV), attributed to their carboxyl and O-functional groups, shifted to a positive value (3.01 mV) upon the addition of Zn2+ (Fig. S5B). This change in zeta potential confirms the interaction between the BCDs and the Zn2+ ions through a ligand-to-metal charge transfer process. The fluorescence quenching constant between the BCDs and the Zn2+ ions was calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q]) [19]. Interestingly, the quenching constants were found to decrease with increasing temperature (333 K, 313 K, and 298 K), with values of 0.54, 0.59, and 0.64, respectively (Fig. 3B). This observation indicates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was a static quenching process. To further elucidate the quenching process, fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. S5C, the fluorescence decay curves of the BCDs remained unchanged after the addition of Zn2+, suggesting a static quenching mechanism. The combined results from the UV–vis absorption spectra and the zeta potential measurements exhibit characteristics consistent with a static quenching mechanism. This implies that the Zn2+ ions form a complex with the functional groups of the BCDs, leading to the observed reduction in fluorescence intensity.

In contrast to the quenching effect observed for the BCDs, the addition of Zn2+ ions to the RCDs resulted in a fluorescence enhancement. There was no change in the fluorescence intensity of the RCDs when exposed to H2O2, cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase, or cysteine (Fig. S5D). Generally, two primary mechanisms are recognized for fluorescence enhancement: metal surface enhanced fluorescence (MEF) or surface plasmon enhanced fluorescence (SPEF), and chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [29–31]. The latter, CHEF, involves the formation of complexes between the surface functional groups and metal ions. Recent studies have suggested that the chelation between surface functional groups and metal ions can reduce internal charge transfer (ICT), enhance photoinduced electron transfer, and consequently increase the emission intensity. In the present study, the observed fluorescence enhancement of the RCDs is likely due to the diminished electron donor capacity of the amino groups when the RCDs chelate with the Zn2+ ions [31]. This process may suppress the ICT transitions while promoting photoinduced electron transfer to the RCDs/Zn2+ complex. Further evidence for the formation of a ground-state complex between the RCDs and Zn2+ ions was provided by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3C). The absorption band at 289 nm declined, demonstrating the formation of this complex. Additionally, zeta potential measurements showed an increase from −18.5 eV to 6.2 eV upon the addition of Zn2+ (Fig. 3D), which attributed to the electrostatic neutralization caused by the chelation process.

Based on the structure of the surface ligands, the interaction between the Zn2+ ions and the amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of the RCDs likely forms a polyatomic ring structure, creating a chelation complex [32]. This process aligns with the CHEF mechanism [33]. The resulting larger conjugate plane and increased rigidity of the chelation complex lead to a more stable structure, which in turn enhances the luminous efficiency. Additionally, this extended conjugated system facilitates the excitation of π electrons, further promoting fluorescence emission. The observed enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the RCDs in the presence of Zn2+ can be primarily attributed to the coordination of the Zn2+ ions with the oxygen and nitrogen atoms of the surface functional groups. This coordination forms a rigid chelation complex, which is the driving force behind the increased fluorescence [34].

Under optimized conditions, we evaluated the performance of our ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for cholesterol detection. Fig. 4 A illustrates the fluorescence spectra obtained with increasing cholesterol concentrations from 0 to 1.0 μM. As cholesterol levels rose, the fluorescence signal of BCDs progressively diminished, while that of RCDs steadily increased. The ratiometric fluorescence signal (F600/F390) demonstrated a positive correlation with cholesterol concentration (Fig. 4B). This relationship was found to be linear, described by the equation F600/F390 = 0.79C + 0.53, with a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9952). This strong linear correlation underscores the reliability and precision of our sensing method. The normal cholesterol concentration range in human serum is typically between 3.9–6.2 mM (150–240 mg/dL). Our method demonstrates a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.014 μM, which is significantly lower than the physiological range of cholesterol in human serum. This value was determined using the standard 3σ/slope method, where σ represents the standard deviation of blank samples. Notably, our LOD is considerably lower than those reported for many previously developed fluorescent sensors, as summarized in Table 1 . The superior sensitivity of our method, evidenced by the low LOD, coupled with the wide linear range and high correlation coefficient, demonstrates the potential of this ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for accurate and sensitive cholesterol detection in various applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of our ratiometric fluorescence platform for cholesterol sensing, we conducted a comprehensive interference study. We examined the effects of common ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +), amino acids (L-methionine, L-arginine, L-threonine, L-cysteine, L-histidine, L-valine, L-lysine, L-tryptophan), carbohydrates (galactose, glucose), and other biomolecules (ascorbic acid, urea, uric acid, and human serum albumin (HSA)) on the ratiometric fluorescence signal F600/F390. As depicted in Fig. 5 , when cholesterol concentration was set at 0.6 μM and all potential interferents were present at a much higher concentration of 10.0 μM, no significant impact on the ratiometric fluorescence measurement was observed. This remarkable selectivity, even in the presence of interfering substances at concentrations higher than cholesterol, demonstrates the robust specificity of our sensing platform for cholesterol detection in complex biological matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical utility of the CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system for cholesterol measurement, the research team conducted experiments using real serum samples. They employed a standard addition technique to verify the system's performance in detecting cholesterol in these complex biological matrices. The experimental procedure involved first diluting the serum samples, then spiking them with known amounts of cholesterol at three different concentrations: 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 μM. These spiked samples were then quantified utilizing the fluorometric method based on the CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system. Table 2 presents the findings from this analysis. The cholesterol levels measured by the proposed method showed strong agreement with the known spiked concentrations, indicating the method's high accuracy in real sample matrices. The recovery rates, which ranged from 97.00 % to 98.67 %, further confirmed the method's reliability. Additionally, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) remained below 3.21 %, demonstrating good precision and reproducibility of the measurements. These impressive accuracy and precision metrics suggest that the CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system is capable of sensitive and reliable cholesterol detection even in complex biological samples. The robust performance observed in these serum tests indicates that this fluorometric method could be a promising tool for cholesterol measurement in clinical or diagnostic applications. Statistical analysis of the percentage recovery data revealed no significant difference between the results obtained from the proposed method and those from previously reported methods [49]. We employed the t-test and F-test to statistically compare the two methods, and the results showed no significant differences at the 95 % confidence level (Table 2). This concordance in recovery rates across methodologies substantiates the reliability and accuracy of the novel fluorometric approach for cholesterol quantification.

In conclusion, this study introduces a novel and highly effective fluorometric method for cholesterol detection using a CYS@Zn/CDs/cholesterol oxidase system. The innovative ratiometric detection strategy, which exploits the dual fluorescence response of two types of CDs to Zn2+ ions, demonstrates significant advantages including high sensitivity, excellent linearity, and successful application to human serum samples with impressive recovery values. The comprehensive characterization of the CDs and elucidation of their interaction mechanisms provide a solid theoretical foundation for the method. The successful implementation in complex biological matrices underscores its potential as a valuable analytical tool in clinical and diagnostic settings. This novel approach represents a significant advancement in cholesterol detection technology, offering improved sensitivity, reliability, and practical applicability, making it a promising tool for both research and clinical applications in lipid analysis and cardiovascular health assessment.

Shaker T. Alsharif: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112618.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
